With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. The image? I've decided to trace, as far as I can, my own occult lineages in photos. Juliet Ashley, whose photo featured in the Magic Monday two weeks ago, was a student of at least four significant occult teachers, and this is the second of them, none other than Carl Jung. (Yes, I know he managed to fool people into treating him as a psychiatrist, but he studied alchemy, filled a book with visionary experiences, and used to cast horoscopes of his patients to help diagnose their issues.  The guy was an occultist, full stop, end of sentence.)  Ashley studied with him in Zurich in the late 1930s, leaving Switzerland just in time to avoid being caught there by the outbreak of the Second World War. Back in the United States, Ashley reworked the symbolism of the orders she headed to fit Jung's mandala teachings, and incorporated his ideas in her teachings in various other ways, some quite subtle.

comments.title: The Northman and Considerations for Daily Divination
comments.content:
Good evening!

As usual, thanks very much for hosting this and sharing the thoughts in your initial post. I think I've asked before about Jung, so this time I'll just say that I look forward to some fuller treatments of Jung's occultism and/or role in helping the modern occultist understand things, but I reckon that's a full post (at least).

To Share: For anyone who does not share our host's lack of interest in moving pictures, I submit for your consideration The Northman, directed by Robert Eggers. Eggers is best known for The Witch and The Lighthouse. This outing is gorgeously shot, largely on location in Iceland and northern Ireland, and the costumes and set design are lavish and firmly historically informed. What led me to include the recommendation, though, is the way the movie treats magical and religious experiences. They're largely shown in ways that can be dismissed as "just in the character's imagination", but that make better sense and are more satisfying if taken as "real" (though there are some scenes that I thought did a clever job of depicting something astral in nature). I also didn't notice anything inconsistent with the documentation we have for Old Norse religion, with a few very specific nods to Ibn Fadlan and other sources.

Fair warning: it's very violent, with some pretty upsetting scenes, since it is, in fact, about actual vikings, with all of the looting, killing, and slaving that involved.

To Ask: JMG, you mentioned in a recent MM that you had gone back to using the Ogham for your daily divination after some time with the Sacred Geometry Oracle. What things do you consider when deciding what method of divination to use for your daily practice, besides "what do I know or am I trying to learn"?

Thanks very much,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Very interesting! This is a total shot in the dark, but in medical practice, 'MSIR' is a commonly used abbreviation for 'Morphine Sulfate-Immediate Release.' This distinguishes it from time-released or slow-release morphine. If it so happens that you have been using morphine or heroin, etc, then Death's advice to get it out of your body sounds like a good idea. There is an extra "I" in there, so its just as likely to be something else. If your impression is that 'MISIR' is a rune or related to one, likely this is the best place to start looking.

With my dreams, I find that I either have their meanings and maybe just have not put them together yet, or that they represent something I will eventually understand--although some of them have taken many years for the meaning to come clear.

If this is a recent dream, try to write down every detail you can remember. Write your observations uncritically, without trying to explain them. What sort of light illuminated your dream? What was the sky or ceiling like? What was the floor or ground like? What were you wearing? It's OK if the floor is flat brown or the sky is plain blue-- Over time, your memory of the dream may drift, change, and accumulate new features in your memory that might not have been there in the original dream. If you have a record of the dream soon after you dreamed it, you can discover what new features grow in over time, and this may also be helpful. A year from now, if you go back and read your dream log, you may be surprised at how different some of your remembered details are from what you wrote.

When you are certain that every detail is recorded, important or not, you can then begin to meditate on the meanings they hold. Was Death being a friend to you? Was that expected? How do you regard ravens in your tradition? In mine, ravens are wise and clever, but this is not necessarily the case in yours.
Pay attention to players that do unexpected things, or objects out of place. What should they be doing? Where should they be?

I wish you all the best for discovering this dream's meanings!
--Turquoise Obtuse Centaur

During banishing, I was leaping over a fire (isn't there a tradition of that around midsummer?).

Invoking Water, I found myself at a river instead of my usual place at the sea, and during banishing, I wept and saw my tears fall into the water. I put my hand into the water, and saw the branches of a weeping willow dip into the water the same way. The song 'Boat on the River' played in my mind, and I remembered that the name of the band was Styx...

Finally, when invoking Spirit Below, the energy ascending from beneath me took the shape of a gigantic tree whose branches reached up to the stars. All kinds of animals lived in it. I couldn't see what species it was, but I knew it was the world tree.

So, this was an amazing experience! But the reason I'm relating it here is because I wanted to ask you about the trees. I saw that trees are included in the version of the SOP on the AODA website, but they're not the same ones I saw. In fact, I had thought that oak would be the tree for Fire and the south, since it's such a regal tree, but apparently, that's not so. Now I wonder if I should start adding the trees I saw in my visualizations (visions?) to my invocations? What do I make of the tree from Spirit Below? Invoke it as the world tree?

Generally speaking, which parts of the SOP can be changed, and which can't (I remember that you said not to change the elemental associations of the directions, for example, and I suppose the same goes for the colors)?

Do they add their own power and blessings, like the elements? What if different animals make their appearance in my visualizations - should I give preference to them over what the AODA chose?


*) With a one-week lapse owed to a crushing migraine. At the end of that week, when the pain was finally fading, I promptly got a visitation from some unfriendly entity - I woke myself up shouting 'this is my house (and you're not welcome)!' I started doing the SOP again on the same day, and on the afternoon of that day, had an amusing synchronicity: I went for some groceries, and the ad on my shopping cart asked me, 'do you want to learn magic?' It was an ad for a course in stage magic. I actually laughed out loud and said, 'yes, but I prefer the real thing'.

Last week I asked a question about ratios and energies, but the question was too general for specific answers, so here goes another version.

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content:
Not JMG, and I can't comment on the specific character of specific natures not the occult nature of ratios, but it might be helpful to think in terms of musical harmony, as the character of ratios can be heard.

Assuming a root note (tonic), another tone at an interval expressed by the 3:2 ratio is a perfect fifth, which sounds harmonious, and other common ratios sound more or less harmonious the 'simpler' they are. 1:1 is clearly a unison, 2:1 an octave higher, 5:3 a major sixth, etc, until you get to more dissonant tones like the leading tone (16/15), which really, really wants to get back to the tonic.

So playing and hearing each of these intervals gives you an idea into their character, also outside of music.

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content: Sure. The √2 ratio (1 to the square root of 2) is the ratio of generation:  it fosters growth and expansion.  The √3 ratio (1 to the square root of 3) is the ratio of integration: it resolves conflicts between polarized forces and brings them into balance. (It's also good for fostering reproduction, since that involves a relationship between two polarized forces.) The √5 ratio (1 to the square root of 5) is the ratio of transmutation -- it enables forces to be raised or lowered from one plane to another, and it is also the ratio that generates the Golden Section.

replies.title: Re: Veil Between Worlds
replies.content:
The concept of "the veil between the worlds" comes from Spiritualism, one of the unacknowledged grandparents of the modern Neopagan movement. It was retro-conned into Celtic tradition, mostly by Americans and non-Celtic Britishers. Outside that subculture, your perception is pretty generally held -- even UFO researchers are aware of "windows," places where anomalous things tend to be seen, and "flaps," periods of time when a lot of them show up.

Me? The world is constantly full of strange things. It's not that there's a veil that gets thinner or thicker, it's that individuals become more or less sensitive to the reality that surrounds us all the time.

replies.title: Re: Veil Between Worlds
replies.content: This matches with Carl du Prel's idea that "The other side and the here and now are not spatially separate, but rather they are divided only by the threshold of perception, accordingly the sensory consciousness only encompasses the earthly existence. The other side is the here and now viewed differently." (The Puzzle of Humanity, 92) He considered occult phenomena to be instances of shifts in the threshold of perception.

replies.title: Re: Veil Between Worlds
replies.content: I would say the idea of the Veil is a universal one (basically the concept of liminality), but the choice of dates is very Celtic (Gaelic to be more precise), though not exclusively so: October and May are of course Samhain/Halloween and Beltane. In Gaelic folk tradition, these were the times when the Faeries/Sidhe moved house. They are strong liminal points, marking the division of the year between Winter (starting on Samhain) and Summer (starting on Beltane). In the Irish mythological tradition most important events also happen at Beltane or Samhain. Given that Gardner, Cochrane and the other founding fathers of Neopagan Witchcraft stressed the celebration of the Gaelic quarters (The Greater Sabbats), it's not surprising that the average neopagan assumes the liminality of the dates. This, of course, is also true for most Celtic polytheists or those traditions taking their inspiration from Scottish or Irish faery lore

replies.title: Re: Veil Between Worlds
replies.content:
The concept of 'the veil' between the physical world and the spirit world is also found in Mormon culture. They'll speak of times when 'the veil is thin.'

'Many times as Latter-day Saints, we’ve heard the expression, “The veil was very thin . . .” preceding or following an experience with a loved one who has passed away.'
https://latterdaysaintmag.com/article-1-8402/

replies.title: Re: Veil Between Worlds
replies.content: No surprises there. The Mormon church emerged in the same place (upstate New York) a couple of decades before the birth of Spiritualism, and so it's not surprising that similar metaphors would be in both.

replies.title: Re: About a demon
replies.content:
Demons have very little power in our world. The one thing they can reliably do is talk clueless human beings into listening to them and following their advice. In recent years a certain number of people have done what the person you mentioned did, and dabbled (or worse) in demonic magic. Traditional occultism teaches that this is a really stupid idea, but these days a lot of people have convinced themselves that the traditional lore is just silly and old-fashioned, and can be ignored by modern, up-to-date mages. They're wrong, and will eventually find this out the hard way, but in the meantime we have to deal with people prancing around boasting about how this or that demon serves them.

No, demons that are summoned aren't unleashed on the world -- they remain bound by divine forces far stronger than they are. They are, however, unleashed on the people foolish enough to listen to them. (Your religion has some very cogent things to say about that!) No god can save a soul that doesn't want to be saved, and when somebody does the atheist trip, insisting that gods don't exist or don't matter, and then starts conjuring demons, they're basically basting themselves in a nice glaze and hopping onto a plate with a sprig of parsley in one hand, while calling out, "Dinner's served!" It's possible that the current situation does have a demonic element, however. As you may recall, a lot of people in 2016 and thereafter were so offended by the election of Donald Trump that they joined in mass spells using evil magic against Trump and his supporters. Many of the published rituals used in those spells included incantations calling upon demons. replies.content:
A commendation ceremony (commendatio) is a formal ceremony that evolved during the Early Medieval period to create a bond between a lord and his fighting man, called his vassal (Latin vassus).

[...]

The junior who was to become the vassal of his senior (seigneur) appeared bareheaded and weaponless as a sign of his submission to the will of the lord and knelt before him. The vassal would clasp his hands before him in a sign of submission, and would stretch his clasped hands outward to the lord.

The lord in turn grasped the vassal's hands between his own, showing he was the superior in the relationship. The vassal would announce he wished to become his "man", and the lord would announce his acceptance. The act of homage was complete.

The physical position for Christian prayer that is thought of as typical today, kneeling, with hands clasped together, originates from the commendation ceremony. Before this time, European Christians prayed in the orans, which is the Latin, or "praying" position that people had used in antiquity: standing, with hands outstretched, a gesture still used today by eastern and some other traditionalist Christians.

https://www.castlesandmanorhouses.com/life_11_commendation.htm

The way I heard it, the vassus is offering their hands to symbolize offering their figurative hands, and the seigneur is enclosing them to indicate protection.

Genuflection traditions are of similar origin.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3) This question is particularly interesting to me. There are hundreds of varieties of 'qigong,' with widely varying methods and purposes - medical, health maintenance, martial, and spiritual. There are many systems of yoga with hundreds of pranayamas and kriyas that affect the subtle body in diverse ways. Likewise, there are many Western magical systems with widely varying methods and purposes. So we should actually compare notes to investigate what's going on and how things affect us energetically, because it's not just qigong that affects your subtle body - all aspects of your lifestyle can affect it, and can give rise to disharmonies and deviations.
My personal opinion and experience - I've noticed in my own practice of Golden Dawn material and its energetics, that the pattern of disharmony excessive Golden Dawn work can create looks like burning out the Yin essence of the body, and rising of Yang - so it's clear that for me excessive Golden Dawn work can cause a rising Yang pattern, too much Yang movement with insufficient stillness, and I would likely get an undesirable result from mixing it with overwork, excessive worry and overthinking, use of stimulants, overuse of Yang tonics, anger and frustration, etc. So for me such work pairs well with Yin nourishing activities, and I believe complicated visualizations and discursive meditation should be balanced by mental relaxation and calm; I would only do something like the Middle Pillar in the most relaxed and gentlest way possible, without any forceful opening, pulling, or pushing of energy.
I've never done work involving the SOP for more than about 10 minutes a day. I found it to have a gentle channel opening effect, with mild stimulation of some centers. But I never did it forcefully or intensely, or for a long duration.
I've found that Daoyin and the kinds of exercises recommended for children or adolescents are pretty safe, because they don't interfere with natural directions of qi flow. Tree Qigong is pretty safe. Moderate practice of simple health or medical qigongs that only encourage qi flow in natural directions, learned under a teacher's instruction, is pretty safe, about as risky as brisk walking, because they are simply slow movements combined with relaxed breathing and awareness of sensations in the body, without any forceful direction of qi. That's just my opinion, and what works for me. There are other qigongs and neigongs designed to develop internal power for fighting, or to accelerate spiritual or psychic development, sexual practices, advanced spontaneous qigongs, and internal alchemical work - this is where great caution is necessary. Some exercises work against each other, which can either weaken the results of both practices, or result in damage to health. Forceful methods for rapid development can be particularly dangerous if undertaken without expert guidance. Certain practices should not be undertaken prematurely, before the channels are open, but people like to skip the foundational work and go to the advanced practices. The advanced spontaneous qigongs can, instead of clearing a major blockage, actually reinforce it and make it worse; so the channels must be open to an extent before such a practice could be done.

Regards,
Josh Rout

comments.title: Selection
comments.content:
JMG,

I'm training for selection here in the UK for a specialist unit in the British army. I was wondering if you had any insight in focusing and conditioning the mind the way I've been conditioning my body?

The selection process takes around a year and involves a lot of moving with weight for long periods of time, sometimes at night while navigating , being deprived of food and sleep etc all while having to be mentally shart.

Any insight on how to maintain mental focus, remove negative thoughts, or at least compartmentalise them etc but also for later down the line to mitigate fear of f`ing up, shooting someone innocent. Any particular occult training systems or pantheon?

Many thanks for any insight.

replies.title: Re: Selection
replies.content: That's not something I've ever seen discussed in Western occult literature. You might want to talk to a martial arts teacher instead -- some of the old martial arts have rules for mental training you might find useful.

replies.title: Re: Selection
replies.content:
Obviously not JMG; but yeah, I have some insights.

1) Be precise in your concept of conditioning. Many people understand conditioning as beating your body into compliance with adaptation coming from the repair. This is wrong. Do not be one of those people. Treat everything as a skill that can be developed and refined; even things like enduring heavy loads or dealing with sleep deprivation are subject to this.

2) There isn't any particular system or pantheon. If you are genuinely agnostic and a certain system is popular in your unit; that may be worth a try. It is also worth noting that the discursive meditation recommended by JMG was very popular in the Anglican tradition. It's gentle and flexible but really builds up nicely over time.

3) Understand what you are trying to do and why. Journaling is a great asset to this. A clear idea of what you want to do and get out of your training can get you through a lot of trouble. If you realize that your motives are less than wholesome or honorable, it's best to get out now and not waste the cadre's time. Related to this is a very specific question for you: do you want to BE as specialist, or do you want to DO the sorts of things that specialist is expected to do. The former is an ego-trip, the latter is a real understanding of your place in the world.

4) Self talk is huge. It isn't enough to refrain from complaining out loud, you shouldn't be complaining to yourself either. If thoughts like that are running through your mind, challenge them. This actually meshes nicely with the discursive meditation mentioned before. Done regularly and systematically with the same formula, this can turn into affirmations; another excellent technique.

5) Visualization might be dismissed in some circles, but it shows up a lot in the context of both elite athletes and elite soldiers. Any good book on sports psychology should talk about it and there has been some surprisingly good research on it over the years. Like any skill, if you aren't happy with your results, break it down into smaller pieces then put it back together after.

6) Rhythmic breathing (in 3 - hold 3 - out 3 - hold 3 - repeat) is a VERY powerful technique once you get used to it. It's commonly taught for dealing with stress but it can be adapted to deal with just about anything. Besides air, you can inhale imagined images or abstract properties. When you hold the air in, try to feel it and anything else circulating through your body. When you hold out, try to feel everything settling back into a neutral state.

7) This is purely physical, but you'll want to make sure you have very solid form in running and standing with good posture. Small problems that'll result in knee pain after a few decades as a civilian will result in stress fractures after a few months as a soldier. It might be worth your time to spend five minutes every day suspending your crown at the top of the head and trying to feel your feet. Weight equally balanced, the ground supporting you, not you resting on the ground.

0) If you don't mind my asking, which unit?

replies.title: Re: Selection
replies.content:
If I may chime in as someone who went through a similar process, mostly to reinforce Taylor's excellent advice:

On 1) and 7), please, please don't underestimate these. The way I ended up learning how to ruck was to arch my lower back, creating a lot of bone-to-bone contact, and leaning forward. This is bad, and came from not having much knowledge or body awareness around posture and the core strength to back it up, and I blame some later back trouble on damage I likely did doing it this way. Kelly Starrett's Deskbound is a fantastic resource for proper posture. Also, I broke both of my feet (stress fractures that I kept rucking on for the remainder of selection). While training, consider some zero-rise and/or thin-soled shoes (and of course, gradually ease into these if you're not used to them). This will help strengthen your bones, but that would be a pretty gradual process. Apparently stress fractures and worse are a lot more common than they used to be because we spend a lot less time barefoot or in leather-soled shoes than folks did a generation or two back.

2) & 3) Journaling was very helpful to me in clarifying why I wanted to do what I was doing, and in my case, it was mostly working through my responses to Stoic philosophy. I didn't know about discursive meditation back then, but I think it would have also helped quite a bit. If you don't have explicitly spiritual beliefs, philosophy can work here too.

4) Absolutely. One technique that can help with this is a "no complaints challenge". Your goal is to either go a fixed amount of time (like two solid weeks), or just to go as long as you can without complaining. Take a rubber band or bracelet and put it on one wrist. Every time you catch yourself complaining, switch wrists and start your count over.

6) Again, can't stress this one enough. I used this technique to get through all of my jumps, and I have continued to use it to deal with various flavors of stress, fear, and other feelings when I feel the need for calm.

Otherwise, I might recommend Jocko Willink, a retired Navy SEAL commander. His book Extreme Ownership is explicitly about leadership, but it includes a section about what traits the men who made it through the selection process had in common, and the core of it was a true desire to serve something bigger and their teammates, not to be individually a badass.

Whatever ends up working for you, best of luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Selection
replies.content:
Oh, and one other thing not magic at all - consider trying to practice land navigation before you are being evaluated on it. Most folks (including myself) are terrible at it at first. Get a compass and a good contour map and go somewhere where you can easily get un-lost (like you can reliably find a road to get back to and/or you have a GPS as a backup) and practice moving from some easy-to-identify-on-the-ground point to another (but ideally not blindingly obvious from afar, like a mountain peak).

For me, the two game-changers were to 1) rotate the map so that "up" is whatever direction I'm heading, rather than always keeping North to the top, and 2) learn to pay attention to the contours of the land around me and associate them with what I was seeing on the map in real time. You can practice 2 with more linear features like roads, walls, or creeks, but it's good to learn to literally "read the lay of the land".

Once you start getting more comfortable, you can do some harder stuff. For example, there are folks who do "Geo-caching" where they leave stuff at a specific Latitude & Longitude and then share the location for other enthusiasts to find (sometimes you take something and leave something of your own, other times it's just an excuse to find something non-obvious).

Apologies if you've already worked on this and it's old hat. Again, good luck!
Jeff

Just a pedantic footnote here ...

Although "Swami Panchadasi" and "Swami Bhakta Vishita"'s books were published by Atkinson, and he undoubtedly was fond of pseudonyms, those two pseudonyms seem to have belonged to Atkinson's friend Claude Alexander Conlin (18870-1945), who was not only an occultist, but a stage magician (Alexander, The Man Who Knows) as well. See now David Charvet's study of him, Alexander--The Man Who Knows (2nd ed., 2007).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: One other pseudonym commonly assigned to Atkinson, viz. "Magus Incognito," was probably an otherwise unknown Clifford Edgar Brooksmith, who renewed the copyright in The Secret Doctrine of the Rosicrucians (1918) during the first half of 1949. This implies that he was most likely the original author, though in principle he might have had the copyright assigned to him by its original holder. The book was sometimes catalogued by librarians under Brooksmith's name instead of Atkinson's, probably on the basis of its copyright.

4) The SoP was created by John Gilbert, who was an expert Cabalist -- a book of his essays on the Tree of Life is in press right now -- and was used intensively in the Golden Dawn order he inherited from Juliet Ashley, which again was full of Cabala. The Judson exercise isn't Cabalistic but it doesn't use much symbolism at all, so is compatible with just about everything. I wouldn't worry about it too much!

comments.title: Gender, Gods, Hellenic SOP
comments.content:
Can you please explain again why the gods and goddesses are aligned the way they are in pagan SOP and Pentagram rituals? How would placing a god across from a goddess in either the SOP or LBRP change things?

I'm thinking of how to arrange the Hellenic deities in either system, and I was specifically thinking about how Sallust assigns the Olympians to the spheres and the elements. I'm sure you know this, but he assigns to Air, Poseidon to Water, Hephaestus to Fire and Hestia to Earth. It would also seem that, if you used his attributions, you would assign Zeus to Spirit Above and Demeter to Spirit Below, since he assigns the latter to Saturn's sphere. Would a SOP based on these attributions work? Or would it blow up?

replies.title: Re: Gender, Gods, Hellenic SOP
replies.content: It would work just fine. The SoP is very flexible. I don't recommend using that for a pentagram ritual, however, because (a) the divine names don't need to relate to the elements, and (b) it works better in the pentagram ritual if all the divine names have four letters each.

comments.title: Advice for Multiple Tarot Decks
comments.content:
I have three tarot decks:

CBD Tarot de Marseille and Oswald Wirth (purchased for the Levi Doctrine and Ritual book club meditation work) and Rider-Waite (purchased for Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose work but have to admit I've yet to start....).

I ended up using Oswald Wirth for meditation for the different Doctrine and Ritual chapters and started divination with the Marseille deck. Even though I am a total novice I feel I get useful information from my readings. I am the person that reached out on this forum a few months ago when I have the Empress appear reversed on three successive readings. I get strong feelings not to ask the card trivial questions and if I have dirtied my hands in any way prior to my intention to read the cards, I get strong feelings to wash them to pay respect.

After reading a recommendation of yours I picked up Mastering the Tarot by Eden Gray. I remember a comment you made that the little booklets that come with the decks are pretty useless.

An observation but also a question is that after trying some readings with the Rider-Waite deck I feel that the Eden Gray book's explanations of the card are specific to that deck, especially as it concerns the minor arcana. Question is could I use Gray's explanations for the Marseille?

Another question is if you feel it is a bad idea to use different decks for divination? I don't mean the extra time to process the different symbolism into the subconscious but one deck may require a different mind set which would interfere with learning a different deck?

replies.title: Re: Advice for Multiple Tarot Decks
replies.content:
1) I know people who use Eden Gray's meanings with the most remarkably odd collection of decks! You should have no problem using them with the Marseilles deck.

2) I recommend learning one at a time, but once you've mastered one you should have no trouble adding another.

replies.title: Re: Advice for Multiple Tarot Decks
replies.content: Thank you Sir!

It would have involved a fake Welsh witchcraft ritual complete with nonsense or joke invocations in Welsh, such as "peidiwch â phoeri yn y cwpan" (don't spit in the cup) when passing the chalice. replies.title: Re: Colin Wilson

He wasn't a jerk like Holzer -- I knew people back in the day who were still irate at the way Holzer reworked whatever he encountered to make it look more scandalous and sell copies of his books. So, as far as I know, he didn't get punked!

comments.title: Elemental Colors Tarot Decks
comments.content:
Archdruid,

Do you have a good reference for describing why certain colors were used to represent the elements? I get red for fire and blue for water but seen references to black to represent earth and yellow for air is something to think upon. I hope for some historical background to help inform my meditations for tarot images and the calling of elements in the Sphere of Protection. Additionally, there may be deeper meanings for red & blue beyond they most resemble the element in question.

I believe the color system is from the Golden Dawn (The Enochian Elemental Tablets?) but the tarot predates the Golden Dawn so they were obviously influenced by older teachings.

replies.title: Re: Elemental Colors Tarot Decks
replies.content: Red and blue were obvious; yellow is the only other primary color, so it usually gets assigned to air in traditions with a strong Cabalistic influence, because in Cabalistic symbolism fire, water, and air are elements, but earth is a muddle of the three. These color correspondences are to some extent arbitrary, so it doesn't greatly matter -- well, except that after a couple of centuries of use, the "tracks in space" are pretty solid.
